input_row.title: Ensure that Resource Locks are set for Mission-Critical Azure Resources
input_row.description: Resource Manager Locks provide a way for administrators to lock down Azure resources to prevent deletion of, or modifications to, a resource. These locks sit outside of the Role Based Access Controls (RBAC) hierarchy and, when applied, will place restrictions on the resource for all users. These locks are very useful when there is an important resource in a subscription that users should not be able to delete or change. Locks can help prevent accidental and malicious changes or deletion.
input_row.rationale: As an administrator, it may be necessary to lock a subscription, resource group, or resource to prevent other users in the organization from accidentally deleting or modifying critical resources. The lock level can be set to to CanNotDelete or ReadOnly to achieve this purpose. • CanNotDelete means authorized users can still read and modify a resource, but they cannot delete the resource. • ReadOnly means authorized users can read a resource, but they cannot delete or update the resource. Applying this lock is similar to restricting all authorized users to the permissions granted by the Reader role. Impact: There can be unintended outcomes of locking a resource. Applying a lock to a parent service will cause it to be inherited by all resources within. Conversely, applying a lock to a resource may not apply to connected storage, leaving it unlocked. Please see the documentation for further information.
input_row.audit: Audit from Azure Portal 1. Navigate to the specific Azure Resource or Resource Group. 2. Click on Locks. 3. Ensure the lock is defined with name and description, with type Read-only or Delete as appropriate. Audit from Azure CLI Review the list of all locks set currently: az lock list --resource-group <resourcegroupname> --resource-name <resourcename> --namespace <Namespace> --resource-type <type> --parent ""  Audit from PowerShell Run the following command to list all resources. Get-AzResource For each resource, run the following command to check for Resource Locks. Get-AzResourceLock -ResourceName <Resource Name> -ResourceType <Resource Type> -ResourceGroupName <Resource Group Name> Review the output of the Properties setting. Compliant settings will have the CanNotDelete or ReadOnly value.
input_row.remediation: Remediate from Azure Portal 1. Navigate to the specific Azure Resource or Resource Group. 2. For each mission critical resource, click on Locks. 3. Click Add. 4. Give the lock a name and a description, then select the type, Read-only or Delete as appropriate. 5. Click OK. Remediate from Azure CLI To lock a resource, provide the name of the resource, its resource type, and its resource group name. az lock create --name <LockName> --lock-type <CanNotDelete/Read-only> -- resource-group <resourceGroupName> --resource-name <resourceName> --resource- type <resourceType> Remediate from PowerShell Get-AzResourceLock -ResourceName <Resource Name> -ResourceType <Resource Type> -ResourceGroupName <Resource Group Name> -Locktype <CanNotDelete/Read- only> Default Value: By default, no locks are set. References: 1. https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/azure-resource-manager/resource-group- lock-resources 2. https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/azure-resource-manager/resource- manager-subscription-governance#azure-resource-locks 3. https://docs.microsoft.com/en- us/azure/governance/blueprints/concepts/resource-locking 4. https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/security/benchmark/azure/mcsb-asset- management#am-4-limit-access-to-asset-management
input_row.impact: There can be unintended outcomes of locking a resource. Applying a lock to a parent service will cause it to be inherited by all resources within. Conversely, applying a lock to a resource may not apply to connected storage, leaving it unlocked. Please see the documentation for further information.

first_opinion.response:
**AUDIT APPROACH:** Automated

**APPROACH DECISION:**
- The control involves checking the existence of resource locks on Azure resources, which can be queried using Azure APIs, Azure CLI commands, and PowerShell.
- The audit field clearly mentions API/CLI commands that can be used for automation.
- There is no mention of needing manual inspection or judgment, which supports the automated approach.

**PROGRAM NAME:** azure_resources_locks_verified

**AUTOMATION DETAILS:**
- The automated program will utilize the Azure Resource Manager (ARM) APIs and Azure CLI commands to check for resource locks on specified resources.
- Resources that need to be enumerated include: 
  - Azure Resource Groups
  - Individual Azure Resources within those groups

- The key Azure CLI command to be used is:
  ```
  az lock list --resource-group <resourcegroupname> --resource-name <resourcename> --namespace <Namespace> --resource-type <type>
  ```

- The PowerShell equivalent checks will be:
  ```powershell
  Get-AzResource | ForEach-Object {
      Get-AzResourceLock -ResourceName $_.Name -ResourceType $_.ResourceType -ResourceGroupName $_.ResourceGroupName
  }
  ```

- The configuration properties/settings to validate include:
  - The name and description of the lock.
  - The lock type, which should be either `CanNotDelete` or `ReadOnly`.

- Pass/Fail criteria:
  - Pass: The resource lock is present with type `CanNotDelete` or `ReadOnly`.
  - Fail: No lock is present, or it does not satisfy the expected lock type.

- Example API endpoint (for direct API querying):
  ```
  GET https://management.azure.com/subscriptions/{subscriptionId}/resourceGroups/{resourceGroupName}/providers/{resourceProvider}/{resourceType}/{resourceName}/providers/Microsoft.Authorization/locks?api-version=2021-04-01
  ``` 

By following this approach, organizations can automate the compliance checks to ensure resource locks are appropriately applied to protect mission-critical resources in Azure.

second_opinion.response:
**APPROACH VALIDATION:** AGREE

- The classification of the audit approach as Automated is correct. The existence and configuration of resource locks can be effectively checked using Azure APIs, Azure CLI, and PowerShell, which supports automation. The control does not require subjective judgment or manual inspection, making automation suitable.

**TECHNICAL ACCURACY:** 9

- The automation details provided are technically accurate. The use of Azure CLI and PowerShell commands to list resource locks is correct. The API endpoint is also accurate for querying resource locks directly via HTTP requests.
- The program name `azure_resources_locks_verified` generally follows the convention but could be more descriptive by including the security intent, e.g., `azure_resources_locks_compliance_check`.

**IMPROVEMENTS:**

- **Program Naming:** Consider renaming the program to `azure_resource_locks_compliance_check` to better align with naming conventions that include the security intent.
- **API Version:** Ensure the API version used (`2021-04-01`) is the latest available to leverage any improvements or changes in the API.
- **Resource Enumeration:** Clarify the enumeration process for all resource types and ensure that the script accounts for all possible resource types and namespaces.
- **Error Handling:** Include error handling and logging in the automated scripts to capture and report any issues encountered during execution.
- **Documentation:** Provide detailed documentation on how to set up and run the automation, including prerequisites and permissions required for executing the scripts.

**CONFIDENCE:** HIGH

- The assessment is well-founded, and the automation approach is technically sound. The use of Azure APIs and command-line tools is appropriate for this control.

**FINAL RECOMMENDATION:** Modify

- Modify the program name to `azure_resource_locks_compliance_check` for clarity and adherence to naming conventions.
- Ensure the latest API version is used, and provide comprehensive documentation and error handling in the automation scripts.

final_decision.response:
**FINAL APPROACH:** Automated

**JUSTIFICATION:**
The control requires verifying the existence and configuration of resource locks on Azure resources, which can be effectively automated using Azure CLI, PowerShell, and ARM APIs. Both AI opinions agree on the feasibility of automation, and the audit steps provided in the compliance control data support the use of automated tools to check for resource locks. There is no need for manual inspection or subjective judgment, making automation the most efficient and reliable approach.

**CONFIDENCE:** HIGH

---

**PROGRAM NAME:** azure_resource_locks_compliance_check

**AUTOMATION DETAILS:**
- **Azure Resource/Service:** Azure Resource Groups and Individual Azure Resources
- **API/CLI Method:** 
  - Azure CLI: `az lock list --resource-group <resourcegroupname> --resource-name <resourcename> --namespace <Namespace> --resource-type <type>`
  - PowerShell: `Get-AzResourceLock`
- **Properties to Check:** 
  - Lock Name
  - Lock Type (CanNotDelete or ReadOnly)
  - Lock Description
- **Pass Criteria:** Resource lock is present with the correct type (CanNotDelete or ReadOnly) and a valid description.
- **Fail Criteria:** Absence of a resource lock or incorrect lock type or missing description.

**DISAGREEMENTS RESOLVED:**
Both AI opinions agreed on the automated approach, with a minor suggestion on the program naming convention. The final decision incorporates the suggested improvement for a more descriptive program name.
